keyword_data.research_summary: As of early 2026, OAuth 2.0 remains the dominant authorization framework, with several critical best practices and vulnerabilities shaping the landscape. Key developments: (1) PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is now considered mandatory for all authorization code flows, including confidential clients — it prevents authorization code interception attacks; (2) The implicit grant flow is effectively deprecated; authorization code + PKCE is the only recommended grant type; (3) Token storage security is critical — access tokens must never be stored in localStorage; httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies are the recommended vehicle; (4) Short-lived access tokens combined with refresh token rotation is the standard; if a refresh token is compromised, the old one is immediately revoked; (5) redirect_uri validation must use exact string matching and must reject URIs not pre-registered; (6) The state parameter is non-negotiable for CSRF protection; (7) Scope validation must strictly enforce that clients cannot escalate their own privileges; (8) JWT access tokens must validate signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and use an allowlist for the algorithm (no none/jwt); (9) Client secrets must never be stored in frontend code or mobile apps; (10) Token revocation endpoints must be implemented so compromised tokens can be invalidated immediately.
keywords: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE best practices 2026
OAuth 2.0 token security vulnerabilities and prevention strategies
OAuth 2.0 redirect_uri validation and state parameter security
